# Transcrição Completa: 2. BrezScales Pierre Million a Month Trophy Talk   [00:00] A standing ovation to our newest million-dollar a month trophy winner, Mr. Brez Scales and Pierre.   [00:08] Thank you. Long ways to go yet, though.   [00:12] Here it goes.   [00:14] Where am I looking?   [00:16] Look at the people in the eyes.   [00:18] All right.   [00:20] Feels good.   [00:22] It does.   [00:24] All right. Well, without further ado, ladies and gentlemen, I'll walk this off the stage for you boys. It's quite heavy.   [00:32] Just so you know, when you do inevitably get your million-dollar month trophy, it's not like those TPT Richard Mills that are super light. This is quite heavy.   [00:44] We equate value with heavy stuff the more that we earn. So when you inevitably get this, I hope you worked out a little bit before you come and get your award.   [00:56] Without further ado, ladies and gentlemen, give it up one more time. Mr. Brez Scales.   [01:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Presentation Opening - Brez Scales"   **NÚMEROS:** N/A   **VISUAL:** Brez no palco com troféu de $1M/mês, audiência aplaudindo, ambiente de evento ao vivo   [01:10] Feel uh little more pressure doing this than I did my uh webinar last month that had 10,000 people on it.   [01:20] But I'm here to talk about the power of organic traffic and how that helped take us from zero to a million a month and specifically zero to 500k a month without touching any paid ads.   [01:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Content = Connection = Conversion"   **NÚMEROS:** $0 → $500K/mês sem ads, $0 → $1M/mês total   **VISUAL:** Fórmula visual simples mostrando progressão linear do conteúdo até conversão   [01:40] But organic content is an important key to your funnel for most people that are focused on that. Content equals connection which leads to conversion.   [01:52] My biggest point with content in specific is creating a connection with your audience is huge. And I feel like I myself, for anyone who knows my content, has seen that I've done a very good job at doing that.   [02:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Without Strategic Content = Leaky Bucket Funnel"   **NÚMEROS:** N/A   **VISUAL:** Metáfora visual de funil vazando sem estratégia de conteúdo   [02:15] Without strategic content, your info product funnel becomes a leaky bucket. Digging into your audience's emotions is huge.   [02:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "What is Organic Content?"   **NÚMEROS:** 4 plataformas principais   **VISUAL:** Logos/ícones das plataformas: TikTok, Instagram, YouTube, Twitter (X)   [02:32] So what is organic content? In the realms of my personal brand, we have TikTok, we have Instagram, we have YouTube, and then Twitter is kind of just my platform to shitpost on.   [02:48] But with TikTok, um, short form, obviously, as well as Instagram, I'm going to dive more into the depths of kind of my strategy behind those two. And then YouTube long form.   [03:05] And my YouTube is a little different from the average person who probably posts on YouTube in here is I pretty much just document my lifestyle as a 20-year-old entrepreneur.   [03:20] And kind of give motivation and don't necessarily even talk much about work on that channel specifically, but I'm going to show you kind of how I funnel that into where I do and how that leads into conversion.   [03:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Top of Funnel: Clip Pages Strategy"   **NÚMEROS:** 43,000+ pessoas na comunidade de clipping, ~2,000 clippers ativos, 50K-7K followers por página   **VISUAL:** Screenshots de múltiplas clip pages mostrando diferentes tamanhos de audiência e engajamento   [03:40] So, top of funnel organic content, the biggest thing for me, which I didn't start till probably about 6 months ago, is clip pages.   [03:52] A lot of you guys probably know Whop, maybe even process through Whop. I run all of my clipping through Whop.   [04:05] And if you look bottom left here, I think Pierre might be covering it a little bit, but I have over 43,000 people in my clipping community who not actively clip my content because a lot of those people just join to see what it's about and don't actually clip.   [04:25] But I have probably, oh man, I would say like 2,000 active clippers. Basically, people who get paid to clip my long form videos into short form and repurpose that onto platforms like Instagram and TikTok.   [04:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Clip Pages Examples"   **NÚMEROS:** Milhões a bilhões de views, 1.3M likes em um clip, 10M+ views estimados   **VISUAL:** Grid de clip pages mostrando variação de followers (50K a 7K), exemplos de viral content   [04:50] So, if we look at the right side, those are some examples of some of the clip pages. You can see some of them 50,000 followers, some of them as small as 7,000 followers.   [05:05] But this helps me spread millions, if not billions of views across short form social platforms.   [05:15] So, this has been absolutely huge for me as I can repurpose all this content as well on my main platforms, which I'm going to talk more about, but this is like basically a brand awareness campaign for a lot of you.   [05:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Modern Day Brand Awareness Campaign"   **NÚMEROS:** 75%+ da audiência é cold traffic   **VISUAL:** Comparação: Clip pages = Brand awareness ads (mas orgânico e viral)   [05:35] I know a lot of I guess raise your hand if you are more than 75% driven off of just cold traffic alone. A lot of people in this room.   [05:48] So this is essentially comparable to like a brand awareness campaign. Now people see these clips and you can see like 1.3 million likes that probably has 10 million views.   [06:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Viral Meme Example - In-N-Out Rental"   **NÚMEROS:** 1.3M likes, ~10M views   **VISUAL:** Clip viral do Brez comendo em carro na In-N-Out, comentários sobre "nice rental"   [06:10] That's like a whole meme behind my personal brand. And dude said I had a nice rental at the car or at the um at In-N-Out. I was eating on my car.   [06:25] And it's like a big meme. And I'm going to get more into that, but all these clips essentially have millions and millions of views across platforms.   [06:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "TikTok Search Feature - 'What Does Brez Do?'"   **NÚMEROS:** 500K likes no vídeo do Aventador   **VISUAL:** Screenshot mostrando busca "what does brez do for a living" aparecendo no próprio TikTok   [06:45] The first thing people see when they see a clip like this is, "Who the fuck is this kid? How does he have these cars? How does he have this lifestyle?"   [07:00] And I don't know if you guys have TikTok or not, but it pulls up a search platform on the video itself. This one specifically has on it, what does Brez do for a living?   [07:15] It's a video of me buying my Aventador and has 500,000 likes. And the most common question across that video is what does Brez do for a living?   [07:30] Which then leads them further into studying Brez, who he is, what he does.   [07:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Clip Strategy Benefits"   **NÚMEROS:** Centenas de clips/dia, $50K/mês investimento em clippers   **VISUAL:** Lista de benefícios: Brand awareness moderna, audiência duradoura, menos pushy, cult-like building, reciclar para main pages   [07:45] So, if this is managed properly, the top of funnel, hundreds of clips posted per day, like I said, a modern-day brand awareness campaign, it's a longer-lasting audience because it's just less pushed on them.   [08:05] It comes more organically to them, and it's essentially more cult-like building. Can recycle all these clips to main short form pages.   [08:20] So, this goes much more strategic. I have people who manage this for me. I'm pretty much hands off with it. I just post across YouTube and they take my content, repurpose it, post it on their platforms, and I can kind of just recycle that content.   [08:40] Now, in specific, I spend probably like $50,000 a month on clippers alone. A lot of people ask me, I've gotten asked this here during lunch if it's worth it.   [08:55] There's no way to track that, unfortunately, but it's definitely worth it. I can assure that.   [09:05] Computer's glitching. Jeremy, boom.   [09:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Middle of Funnel: Short Form Content"   **NÚMEROS:** 550K TikTok followers, 650K Instagram followers, dezenas a centenas de milhares de views/post   **VISUAL:** Screenshots dos perfis TikTok e Instagram com métricas de engagement   [09:20] So, getting into middle of the funnel is short form content. So, on TikTok, I have 550,000 followers. On Instagram, I have 650.   [09:35] With TikTok, this is kind of my platform. I honestly kind of just shitpost some of these videos. Some of them are strategic, but you can see each one of these videos is pulling in tens of thousands of views, if not hundreds of thousands of views.   [09:55] So, I'm reaching millions of people per day just on TikTok alone.   [10:05] Instagram, however, the algorithm on Instagram, for some reason, it seems easier to actually pull views in. Um, that platform as well, basically my strategy with that is just repurposing the content alone that I use for my clippers and posting on Instagram.   [10:25] TikTok is where I actually like post raw videos. So I'll show more examples of that in a second.   [10:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Short Form Content Styles"   **NÚMEROS:** 4 categorias principais + 1 honorável menção   **VISUAL:** Grid mostrando 5 tipos: 1. Educational, 2. Motivational, 3. Lifestyle, 4. Humorous, 5. Cars   [10:40] But short form content number one, I guess this is going to be kind of the styles of short form content.   [10:50] So with educational content, with the kind of level of scale my personal brand has gotten to, a lot of guru allegations come with that.   [11:05] So, as I've kind of taken it to scale, I've kind of made the pivot to talk less about what I do for a living and the educational side of things on my main platforms and repurpose that over to second channel.   [11:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Educational Content → Second Channel"   **NÚMEROS:** N/A   **VISUAL:** Screenshots do segundo canal YouTube e TikTok focados em conteúdo educacional/business   [11:30] So, you can see like my second YouTube channel here is where I talk more about kind of what I do for a living as well as my second TikTok and just give advice.   [11:45] This is basically me answering questions through comments, me posting videos, going over tutorials of what I do for a living, just talking more about the business side of things in general.   [12:05] Now, we use every single one of these short form videos in our Hammer Them campaigns as well as with the long form videos, we use every single one as contextual back-end selling assets.   [12:25] So those get recycled as well as the top of funnel content does like I talked about earlier.   [12:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "2. [14:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Fiji Water Grandpa Meme - Viral Inside Joke"   **NÚMEROS:** 1M+ likes, Fiji Water respondeu oficialmente   **VISUAL:** Vídeo viral sobre "avô dono da Fiji Water", resposta oficial da Fiji negando afiliação   [14:45] Apparently I have the same last name as the guy who owns Fiji Water and someone commented that. I made a video replying to it and it went like mega viral.   [15:00] Like this was a video that my friend made and then my clippers clipped it. It got over a million likes.   [15:15] So, this was like a huge meme across my personal brand that supposedly my grandpa owns Fiji water, which actually turns out it was really good because it kind of deflected the course seller allegations.   [15:35] Um, but I just played into it and even Fiji ended up replying to it saying they have no affiliation with me, which was kind of funny. So, I just keep playing into it and it works.   [15:50] It creates like a deep connection with the audience as well as it's just humorous. It goes viral. Like it's good.   [16:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Brez Needs Turkey - Hair Loss Meme"   **NÚMEROS:** 20 anos, queda de cabelo rápida, top comment em quase todos os vídeos   **VISUAL:** Meme recorrente sobre transplante capilar na Turquia, Brez abraçando o humor   [16:10] Two, another funny story is I'm only 20 and my hair is rapidly thinning and people love to make fun of me for it. So, I play into it on a mass scale.   [16:25] I'm actually going to uh to Turkey on Monday. I only have a layover there. I'm not actually getting a hair transplant, but I uh I play into it on a mass scale.   [16:40] People love it. Like the top comment on almost every one of my videos is Brez needs to go to Turkey. Um I don't take offense to it. I just play into it and it works. He sells a trading offer. Um, but people love when we collab. [17:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Honorable Mention: CARS"   **NÚMEROS:** Aventador, GT3 RS, 400M views em 1 mês no GT3 RS   **VISUAL:** Compras de carros viralizando massivamente, clippers enlouquecendo   [18:00] Honorable mention would be cars. I'm a huge car guy. Um, this obviously works very well for personal brand buying a car. Everybody's like, "Who the hell is this kid? How did he buy this car?"   [18:20] Probably people in this room that have experienced that. Um, but I bought an Aventador. I have some other cars. I recently bought a GT3 RS.   [18:35] Every single time that I buy a car, it goes insanely viral. My clippers go insane on it. I'm sure there's probably people in the room that have seen those clips.   [18:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Bottom of Funnel: Long Form Content"   **NÚMEROS:** 20-60 minutos/vídeo, 100K-400K views/vídeo   **VISUAL:** Funil completo: Clip pages → Short form → Long form YouTube (objetivo: conexão profunda)   [19:00] But now, bottom of funnel content. So, all of this obviously people start by seeing my content through clip pages. They start by seeing my content through the short form that I post, which then the whole goal is to funnel them to long form content.   [19:20] Obviously, you guys know when someone sits down and they watch a 20, 30, 40, 50 minute YouTube video about Brez Scales and him experiencing life as a 20-year-old entrepreneur, they're going to be more and more curious.   [19:40] What the hell does this kid do for a living? They're going to just build that connection with me, and I'm going to be able to establish that connection with them.   [19:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Main Channel Strategy"   **NÚMEROS:** 1 soft CTA por vídeo (meio do vídeo), 100K-400K views/vídeo   **VISUAL:** Estratégia de CTA minimalista: apenas 1 soft CTA para segundo canal, zero menção direta de ofertas   [20:00] So, in my long form content, the whole goal of those videos is not even to throw anything in their face, just solely to build that connection with them.   [20:15] Simply all I do in those videos is one soft CTA about halfway through to my second channel. I don't even CTA anything that I sell anymore.   [20:30] Now it started off a little bit at lower scale as I did. But now with the scale it's gotten to where every video is getting one, two, three, 400k views, it can bring a lot of hate from that.   [20:50] So, I drive that over to my second channel, which is where I solely just post educational content, talk more about what I do for a living, give free value.   [21:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Main Channel Content Types"   **NÚMEROS:** 20 minutos a 1 hora/vídeo, 1 vídeo a cada 5 dias   **VISUAL:** Lista de conteúdo: Férias com amigos, agency team, comprando/dirigindo carros, life advice, crashando Aventador   [21:15] Now, my main channel, like I said, whole goal with this is just to pretty much document my lifestyle, have people be entertained.   [21:30] I kind of classify myself more so on this channel now as like an influencer rather than like a business YouTuber, even though my content originally kind of started as on this channel posting more about what I was doing for a living.   [21:50] But you can only get so many views with that. So now it's me going on vacation with my friends. It's me with my agency team. It's me buying cars. It's me driving my cars and giving life advice. It's me crashing my Aventador.   [22:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Main Channel Philosophy"   **NÚMEROS:** N/A   **VISUAL:** Características: Authentic/Real/Raw, Ressonância profunda, Personalidade crucial, Pouco work/life balance mostrado, Deixar pessoas em AWE   [22:20] Um, and my main channel or sorry, we're still on my main channel. Videos 20 minutes to an hour long. Huge nurturing process there. Authentic, real, and raw. Resonates with the audience on a deep level.   [22:40] Personality is huge. I definitely would say not every single person out there could build a personal brand if they want to because they don't necessarily have the personality for it.   [23:00] I show a tiny bit of work life balance within my main channel vlogs as it's kind of drives curiosity from the viewers and then leave people in awe over you as a person.   [23:20] One simple again one simple CTA to the second channel.   [23:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Second Channel Strategy"   **NÚMEROS:** 1 vídeo/semana, VSSL funnel direto   **VISUAL:** Comparação: Main channel (soft CTA, free training opt-in) vs. Second channel (value-driven, harder CTAs, direct application)   [23:35] So on my second channel much more value driven. The more value you give the better. I don't think there's any such thing as too much free value.   [23:50] Harder CTAs than top of funnel or middle funnel. We on this channel run a direct VSSL funnel. So this is straight just application to my program.   [24:10] As we're on the main channel, we run a free training opt-in. So this is the deepest form of nurturing.   [24:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Content Strategy Framework"   **NÚMEROS:** N/A   **VISUAL:** Framework estratégico: Cada conteúdo mapeado para objetivo de funil específico (TOFU/MOFU/BOFU)   [24:30] Now content isn't random. Content strategy is better than just shitposting. Map each piece of content to a funnel goal.   [24:45] The piece of content itself. Ask yourself, what is the goal with that piece of content? The funnel stage of the content, whether it's top of funnel, middle of funnel, bottom of funnel.   [25:05] Where are you trying to take those people after they see that piece of content within that stage, and then repurpose the content across platforms and funnel stages.   [25:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "The Numbers - Organic Traffic Breakdown"   **NÚMEROS:** 1 bilhão de views (estimativa), 60M profile views/30 dias (Instagram), 100K novos followers/30 dias, 32K novos subs YouTube, 650K novos viewers YouTube, 10K subs segundo canal/28 dias, 550K total viewers segundo canal   **VISUAL:** Funil de conversão detalhado mostrando progressão de bilhões de views até subscribers pagantes   [25:30] So, the numbers, let's say in the past couple of months, I've attracted in a billion views, which is probably pretty accurate from clippers, short form, whatever.   [25:50] Let's say 16% of those people end up going to my personal profile, which sounds generous, but if we look at the numbers of my profile views in the last 30 days, it's 60 million people.   [26:10] 60 million people have clicked on my Instagram profile alone. That's not TikTok, that's not YouTube, anything else.   [26:25] So, let's say out of those 60 million people, 100,000 of those people convert to actual followers, which there's the statistic in the last 30 days.   [26:40] And better yet, they convert to YouTube viewers or subscribers, which in the last 30 days on YouTube, I've gained 32,000 subscribers and 650,000 new viewers.   [27:00] So, then they convert to second channel viewers. In the last 28 days, I've gained 10,000 subscribers on my second channel. Uh I don't have the statistic of how many new viewers, but about 550,000 total.   [27:25] And that's the importance of organic traffic.   [27:35] Now, we got to 500K a month without any sort of paid traffic, but my partner Pierre, who works the sales and marketing side of things, as well as paid ads, is going to talk more about that. Anybody got questions for Brez?   [28:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Q&A Session - Brez"   **NÚMEROS:** N/A   **VISUAL:** Sessão de perguntas e respostas ao vivo   [28:30] [Pergunta sobre planejamento de lifestyle content]   [28:35] Lifestyle content. Like are you just like day in the life? Do you guys sit down and plan out what you want to do before the day or you just have a camera follow you around?   [28:50] For me, raw, real, and authentic has been huge. So, there's days like Thursday, I had a good idea for a video where I had ChatGPT plan me a day in the life.   [29:10] I told it, plan me a cool day in the life as a 20-year-old millionaire. And it had me start with boxing in my backyard. Then it had me going shopping. Then it had me going to lunch. Then it had me going in a helicopter. Then it had me doing a whole bunch of other cool shit.   [29:35] So, that was all planned. But other than that, no, not really. It's all just spontaneous. I just go with the flow unless it's like me buying a car. I kind of plan that out a little bit with my filmer. I have a full-time filmer.   [29:55] Um, we'll kind of plan out like little segments of the vlog for me to do a motivational yap that my clippers can clip up.   [30:10] Cool. Other than that, pretty pretty raw.   [30:20] [Pergunta sobre CTAs em canal pessoal vs. business]   [30:25] I have a personal YouTube channel and then a business YouTube channel. So, when we're sending social media clips, would you have the personal one? Where would we do the more aggressive kind of CTAs to book a call or to watch a VSSL? Would that be on the personal one or the business one?   [30:50] Yes. So, that would be second channel. Like for me, for example, on my main channel, the only CTA I ever do is one, and it's only to CTA to my second channel.   [31:10] Second channel is where I'm more direct, tell them to go to the link in the description, but other than that, it's just like in my main video, like, oh, I posted a new video on my second channel. If you guys want to learn more about what I do, like, go check out my second channel.   [31:35] So, what are you posting on your main at the moment?   [31:40] Nothing.   [31:42] Why?   [31:45] Just been it's just been dormant for a couple of years because we've been doing well in other areas, but now I'm wanting to reinvigorate the YouTube strategy.   [32:00] Yeah. So the whole I mean the whole goal with the main channel is kind of to just drive more traffic, get more views as where the second channel is kind of to just have views come from the main channel with a more direct CTA.   [32:20] [Pergunta sobre frequência de posting]   [32:25] How many times are you posting long form and short form content per week?   [32:35] Yeah. Um, so YouTube, it's once every 5 days, which gets to be a lot considering they're like 30 to hour long videos, but it's gotten to the point now where I'm pretty hands-off with my own marketing agency, pretty hands-off with the sales and marketing side of my offer.   [33:00] Um, so once every five days on YouTube, short form, three times, two to three times a day on Instagram, sometimes four if I have four posts worth posting.   [33:20] Um, TikTok, it's probably one to three times a day. Like TikTok I haven't even posted yet today because I haven't been doing anything. But um yeah.   [33:40] Is that for both the YouTube channels every five days?   [33:45] Oh, so the second YouTube is once a week. Once a week. But that I actually have a team behind that that runs that. They used to run the ideation, the scripting, the thumbnails, everything.   [34:05] But I actually just fired them and started working with a new editor because I'm starting a new series where I basically take someone from my program. It's like Alex Hormozi style and consult them live and then they edit it up and like... Yeah. But once a week on the second channel.   [34:30] [Pergunta sobre equipe criativa]   [34:35] You have a full-time videographer. You obviously have the clippers, but what does your creative team look like?   [34:45] Don't really have one. It's kind of all just me. And I think that's why I've done so well with personal branding is because from the get-go, I didn't try too hard at it.   [35:05] And I think people really resonated that and liked that because it was very raw. It was very authentic. It started as just me carrying a camera everywhere I went.   [35:25] But as it evolved, I needed to get better production style. So I hired a full-time filmer. Now I have the clippers, obviously, but from the get-go, it never really was that. So it evolved over time.   [35:45] But what about like editors and...   [35:50] Yeah. Yeah. So I have I mean there's 43,000 people inside of my Whop which they join the Whop and then there's a course in there on how to clip my content and then they get paid per view.   [36:10] So it's all commission based. They get paid roughly like $2 per thousand views. So I'm spending like $50,000 a month on clippers alone.   [36:30] Um but yeah, as far as the creative side of things goes, it's kind of all just me.   [36:40] [Pergunta sobre ROI: clippers vs. Facebook ads]   [36:45] Do you think uh like with the actual revenue per dollar would that be better spent on Facebook?   [36:55] It depends on the person. Think of someone like Iman for example. No matter what you post of Iman, it's Iman's face. It's going to go viral.   [37:15] But for someone whose face isn't viral like that, it has to get to a point where their face can go viral in order for it to be worth it.   [37:30] I see a lot of people like try to do clippers, but people are seeing those videos and have no idea who the fuck that person is behind the clip and they don't care about it.   [37:50] So, it depends on the person. For someone like me, obviously, it's worth it. For someone who doesn't have that virality already, I think it would probably be better spent on Facebook. Yeah.   [38:10] [Pergunta sobre estratégia específica com clippers]   [38:15] Is there a specific strategy with the clippers like to where to get the content from?   [38:25] Yeah. So, we'll do different campaigns and stuff with that. Um we'll launch like a campaign that'll be like get paid $2 per thousand views to clip this specific video.   [38:45] Let's say when I bought the GT3 RS, it was insane the amount of views that we were pulling in. We pulled in like 400 million views in a month. It was going insanely viral.   [39:10] Um so, for that, we throttled the CPMs up. Pay them went from like getting paid a dollar per thousand views to $2.   [39:25] That way, everyone who clips on Whop for other creators sees, oh, Brez is paying $2 per thousand views now, and this is automatically going viral. I can make money so easily.   [39:45] So, they're all jumping over to my Whop to come clip for me. But, what was the question in specific?   [39:55] The strategy. So, do the clippers go and make another Instagram page that relates to...   [40:05] Yeah. Yeah. I can probably go... Oh, we got Pierre's up now. But, they each make their own account. They grow their own account. They get different rewards and stuff off hitting follower goals as well.   [40:25] But um then I just download that content and repurpose it onto my main.   [40:35] And do they drive it to a link on their Instagram page which is our company link or their own?   [40:45] They'll I mean people are seeing my face and my name as well and naturally they kind of fall into seeing my own pages or they'll look up.   [41:00] You know how I showed that example of like at the bottom of the TikTok it said what does Brez do for a living? They'll click on that search, my profile will be the first one that comes up.   [41:20] So, it kind of all is like happening naturally, which is why in my opinion, I think for me in specific, it performs better than a brand awareness campaign because it's less like of a call to action. It's more organic. It's more natural. So, yeah.   [41:45] [Pergunta sobre turning point da marca pessoal]   [41:50] What would you say was like the biggest turning point for your personal brand?   [42:00] I would say that's a good question. I would say... was that crash your car?   [42:15] That was pretty viral when I crashed it. Um probably when I started YouTube because originally I started off just posting short form content, TikTok, Instagram.   [42:35] My whole goal with that was just to drive more leads to my agency. That was like three years ago. And then eventually it kind of evolved into people being more curious about what I was doing for a living.   [42:55] My lifestyle started getting cooler. So I started making cool lifestyle content. I was always really into cars but never had the money to afford them until two years ago.   [43:15] Um, so I started buying cars and making a lot of car content. But ultimately, I would say it was probably when I kind of found my niche on YouTube and kind of developed a new niche within like the younger audience business content of like raw authentic vlogging.   [43:40] So, it was just like documenting my lifestyle as a 20-year-old kid who makes a lot of money, I guess, and people just loved it. So, I just kept going with it and kept evolving it. But, yeah.   [44:00] [Pergunta sobre adaptar estratégia para público mais velho]   [44:05] So, I know you work with a lot of younger kids. How would you take what you do now to maybe somebody that's in their mid-30s or 40s?   [44:20] Yeah, that's a good question. Um, obviously for me, it's I feel like it's much easier because they can resonate with me a lot more and that's the majority of people on social media consuming that content.   [44:40] But even look at even look at someone I don't know who's someone older that's doing really well right now.   [44:50] [Alguém menciona alguém, mas áudio não claro]   [44:55] Yeah, I don't see much of his stuff. I know who that is. But I mean there it's kind of the same strategy really. It's just a matter of getting to the point where people see you and are entertained by you because of who you are and what you do and what you preach.   [45:20] So for me it's different because it's like they can resonate with me a lot more because I'm their age. They see me as like a normal ass kid who has a lot of money.   [45:40] Like the shit that I post, the humorous content, like I'll post the dumbest stuff ever and they love it. So it's a different game for sure. All good.   [46:00] [Pergunta sobre vender produtos físicos]   [46:05] Yo, have you tried adjusting or not even adjusting with any point selling product and not necessarily offer business?   [46:20] Yeah, actually. So funny story. Um, originally like I started posting content because I owned a clothing brand and that was my goal with content was to drive traffic to my clothing brand.   [46:40] And that's what got me into running ads for streetwear brands. That's like the offer that I sell. Um, was that I signed my first couple of clients and then as soon as I realized there was more money in that, I started posting content about the results that I was getting people and then that just evolved into the vlogging stuff.   [47:10] But I did actually try selling a product a little while back. I started a watch brand. I was pretty hands-off with like the creative side of things, just kind of giving ideas and having someone run it for me. And it did well. It sold out.   [47:35] Um, but I think I'm going to kind of evolve that into a jewelry brand at some point. I would love to launch some sort of product.   [47:50] Look at somebody like Iman. He's doing energy drinks and glasses. And I think I'm at the point, if not getting to the point, where I could sell basically any product, and I think people would probably buy it.   [48:15] It's just when is the right time to do it, you know? I don't want it to be framed as like trying to sell my audience too hard. So, it would have to be the right thing and if the right play falls in a lap, I'd do it. But it's just a matter of when, I guess.   [48:40] [Pergunta sobre transição para paid ads]   [48:45] How do you transition from organic to paid?   [48:50] That's a good question. I mean, paid is nothing but gas on the fire for the organic that's already there because you have all of those people who know Brez, who love Brez, who are entertained by Brez, and they would buy something from him.   [49:15] It's just a matter of getting it more in front of their face. So, I wouldn't even say it's necessarily like a transition. I would say it's more so just throwing gas on the fire of what you've already done organic.   [49:35] Like for us, it was so easy to start running paid. What? Four months ago? Three?   [49:45] Yeah, like three months ago.   [49:50] Like three months ago. Our results were insane and they still are because there's all those people who are coming in top of funnel. It's just a matter of retargeting them with a more direct CTA.   [50:10] Was there anything about starting paid that was tougher than you thought or was it just like smooth sailing because of organic?   [50:20] No, I mean I've been running ads for like three years. I don't run the ads for my offer. That's what he does. Um, but more of a question for him, I guess. But it was pretty smooth sailing, bro.   [50:40] It was just a matter of like simple ass ad creatives. Like nothing too hyped, too flashy. Pretty much just like me super raw.   [50:55] Like our ad creative style is like super raw. It's me holding the camera like talking over a script that is decently direct but not like too direct and simple CTA at the end.   [51:15] Yeah. You're cooking up my high school friends with those. Yeah. Any more?   [51:30] [Pergunta sobre ROI estimado dos clippers]   [51:35] Yeah. If you were to put like an ROI on like I know you said it's pretty hard to say, but like what do you say like two, three, four more?   [51:50] I mean it. No, it's got to be more, bro. Like when I bought the GT3 RS, it was insane the amount of virality that that was bringing me.   [52:10] And it's hard to tell because a lot of those people will see a Brez Scales clip and would never buy anything. Like it could take them a year to buy from me.   [52:30] Um, if I had to guess, I would say probably like five, six, seven, eight, I'd say. But it's not going to be for every person, you know?   [52:50] It's different because you could be spending a lot of money and driving traffic. It's shit traffic and you have to funnel them properly because they can see those clips but if they're not going anywhere to further get nurtured and end up buying it's not worth it.   [53:15] [Pergunta sobre como funilar clippers para conversão]   [53:20] So how do you funnel them like properly besides search? Do they have their profile like their bio?   [53:30] Good question. Yeah. So for example, we ran a webinar last month. Couple days before the webinar, I made a post in the Whop requiring every single clipper to post a story saying that I'm running a webinar.   [53:55] Um, that's like super super direct. Usually I don't do stuff like that for a webinar I did. Um, other than that, it kind of happens naturally, bro.   [54:15] That's kind of the point of clipping is that it's super natural and it's super like smooth and not like throwing it into their face.   [54:30] They just see the clip and they're like, "Oh, damn. This kid seems cool. Like I'm going to go look him up on YouTube or naturally I pop up on their YouTube homepage and they're like, "Oh, I've seen this kid on TikTok scrolling before. Like, let me check out his video." And then they're hooked.   [54:55] So, do you say like your CTR on YouTube is like massively high?   [55:05] I don't to be honest with you, I don't look at my YouTube analytics whatsoever. Um, but probably I would say probably, but I don't focus on that necessarily.   [55:25] I think too many people get mental masturbation by looking at things like YouTube analytics. Have a good thumbnail, have a decent title, and make good content. It's literally as simple as that.   [55:50] There's no thing that I could tell you that'll make something click in your mind to allow you to make good content and get things like good click-through rates. I don't look at any of that.   [56:15] Now, on the second channel, the YouTube team that I had, they look at that stuff. Gives them an idea of like viewer retention and what they need to include at the beginning of the videos, the end of the videos, when to throw the CTA, and it makes more sense for something like that.   [56:40] But for, you know, lifestyle vlogging, I don't think it's that deep.   [56:50] All right. Anymore.   [57:00] [Pergunta sobre incluir app link nas descrições]   [57:05] Do you guys ever experimented with including like your app to your Instagram and like the descriptions of the post? [57:30] Um, but I feel like, excuse me, I feel like majority of people who are seeing my clips have already seen me before and they already like know who I am. Um, but yeah, they'll put that in their bios and stuff.   [57:50] Anything else?   [57:55] I give up. Appreciate all. Everybody who doesn't know Pierre, he's Brez's marketer and he's going to give the lessons of the paid advertising side of this deal.   [58:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Pierre Takes Over - Paid Advertising Side"   **NÚMEROS:** Parceria começou março 2024 (4K followers na época), primeiro $1M mês em maio, último mês $1M+ novamente   **VISUAL:** Transição para apresentação de Pierre sobre paid ads e scaling   [58:20] So me and Brez actually started this offer back in um what was it? I don't know like a year and a half ago maybe.   [58:35] March maybe.   [58:40] Yeah, March of uh 2024. Uh he literally had like 4,000 followers at the time. So we started from nothing and since then obviously he's blew up a ton.   [59:00] Um in May we hit our first million dollar month. We did $1.17 million CC and last month we did just over a million dollars.   [59:20] Um, we're going to do it again this month hopefully. Uh, long story short, like I said, he blew up like crazy.   [59:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "$0 → $500K/Month Timeline"   **NÚMEROS:** $4,800 offer (antes $4,000), pico de $544K em janeiro (antes de entrar no Jeremy's mastermind)   **VISUAL:** Linha do tempo de crescimento, estagnação em $400-500K por 4 meses   [59:40] Um, from zero to 500k a month was us just really uh just doing more of whatever works. So, we're running a $4,800 offer. Actually, at the time back then it was $4,000.   [01:00:05] Um, we just run an opt-in VSSL. So, it's like a free training type of thing on his main channel. We run uh like learn what I do for free type of thing. It's an opt-in. It's a VSSL. Really, really casual.   [01:00:25] Um, and yeah, goes to type form app and then it's not a scheduler. They never get access to a scheduler. They actually just get it's like uh if they're qualified, they say we're going to reach out to you.   [01:00:45] It's a little video of him saying we're going to reach out to you. We figured we realized that that works best uh for this type of audience. They're really young and they need to have their hand held through the whole process.   [01:01:05] So, we never give them access to the scheduler. Um, except we're trying to implement it now through paid as a test. But, um, yeah, we have our setters reach out immediately.   [01:01:25] Our speed to lead is probably is literally insane. Um, everyone gets called in under like 30 seconds. So, um, and then they go to a closing call and that's that.   [01:01:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Stuck at $400-500K for 4 Months"   **NÚMEROS:** Pico: $544K em janeiro, estagnação por 4 meses até maio   **VISUAL:** Gráfico de plateau mostrando estagnação antes do breakthrough de $1M   [01:01:50] Our peak month, uh, was in January. We did $544,000 and that's when I joined Jeremy's mastermind.   [01:02:10] Um, back then, looking back at it now, based on what I know, I think we should have been, he should have been doing a million a month a long time ago. Um, it was just honestly just a skill issue to be honest.   [01:02:30] But we got stuck between four and 500k a month for literally four months until May. Um, and it was us just doing the same thing. We just didn't know uh how to push to the million-dollar a month mark.   [01:02:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "4 Reasons We Hit $1M/Month"   **NÚMEROS:** 4 mudanças principais   **VISUAL:** Lista numerada: 1. Fizemos a matemática, 2. Elevamos padrões da equipe, 3. Fechamos leads 2-3 estrelas (não só 4-5), 4. Múltiplos mecanismos de conversão   [01:03:00] And so these are the four things that I would pay uh tribute to that got us to million dollars in CC.   [01:03:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Reason #1: We Actually Did The Math"   **NÚMEROS:** $1M/mês = $33.3K/dia, 85% show rate, $1,000 avg CC/call taken, 40 calls/dia necessários, 476 deals/mês, $4,800 price point   **VISUAL:** Breakdown matemático completo mostrando inputs necessários para $1M/mês   [01:03:20] Reason number one, we actually did the math like before. Everything we were doing was like, "All right, we're doing good. Let's just book more calls, let's just make more money."   [01:03:40] Like we never actually sat down and just did the math on like what does it actually look like to make a million dollars um in a month.   [01:04:00] So like step number one in hitting any goal is to simply figure out if it's mathematically possible. Do I even have enough reps? Do I have enough setters?   [01:04:20] Is the price of the offer even probable in any way to get to a million dollars a month? Those are the kind of things we had to figure out.   [01:04:35] Shout out Jeremy's old mastermind talk called Money Math. That that shit changed the game for us. Oh, I don't know what month was that? What mastermind was that?   [01:04:55] Yeah, a long time ago. Anyways, I went back and watched that and I was like, "Oh my god, this makes sense. I'm retarded."   [01:05:15] Um, so we actually did the math. A million dollars a month is 33.3K a day. Our show rate was 85%.   [01:05:30] Um, we do like a really hard qualification process. We kind of have to with this demographic. And our average CC per call taken was $1,000.   [01:05:50] So like per every call we took, if we took 30 calls in a day, we were averaging out to collecting $1,000 per call taken.   [01:06:10] Um, so we needed to book 40 calls per day on average to make 33.3K a day. And our average deal size was around $2,100, meaning we needed 476 deals closed a month to make a million dollars um with that $4,800 uh price point.   [01:06:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Getting Creative with Cash Collection"   **NÚMEROS:** 476 deals necessários/mês, foco em AOV   **VISUAL:** Estratégias: Pagamentos recorrentes, múltiplas formas de coletar cash upfront, aumentar AOV   [01:06:45] So just actually breaking down those numbers made us realize that we need to do other things to make that more probable.   [01:07:05] Like we weren't focusing on recurring payments at all. We were just focusing on front-end deals, which made our life insanely hard.   [01:07:20] So, we got really really creative with different ways to collect more cash up front. Um, and on top of that, um, get recurring payments.   [01:07:35] Like, we implemented a bunch of different stuff to just to increase AOV pretty much.   [01:07:50] So, doing the math and visually showing sales reps the numbers they need to hit and the inputs they need to do made all the difference.   [01:08:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "KPI Strategy: Build in Buffer"   **NÚMEROS:** 40 calls/dia necessários → KPI real: 60 calls/dia   **VISUAL:** Estratégia de buffer: dobrar padrões para criar margem de erro   [01:08:15] Um, on top of that, the 40 calls booked per day was not the KPI. The KPI was 60 calls per day. And this gave us room for error, which was huge.   [01:08:35] Like we just doubled everyone's standards. Um and anything one of the main things that I would say uh led us to like being able to raise everyone's standards, we just put up with less.   [01:08:55] Like we just did not put up with anything. We stopped putting up with um anyone that wasn't hitting KPI, people showing up to meetings late, all that type of shit was just not tolerated anymore.   [01:09:20] And we really really got buy-in from everyone on the team uh to hit this goal. And I think just showing them the numbers and how probable it was, especially with our front-end traffic, like this dude was cooking on content. We're getting so much traffic.   [01:09:45] There's no way we shouldn't be making a million dollars a month. And when you actually visually show that to your team, it's like, "Oh my god, this makes so much sense." Um, yeah.   [01:10:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Reason #2: Raising Team Standards & Buy-In"   **NÚMEROS:** N/A   **VISUAL:** Cultura de accountability, objetivo comum claro, não sobre dinheiro individual mas meta coletiva   [01:10:10] Reason number two, like I just talked about, is just raising team standards and getting buy-in on a common goal.   [01:10:25] Like we always had a goal of like making more revenue than the month before, but it was never like a very very clear thing um that everyone was bought into.   [01:10:45] Like it wasn't about making anyone money. It wasn't about like certain closers making 10k a month or 15k a month or me making 100k a month or 200k a month. It was like we need to make a million dollars a month.   [01:11:10] Um and we really really got buy-in and I think that played a huge role. Uh first I think you need to believe it's possible. Then you need to be able to convince others they can do it too.   [01:11:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Challenge: No One Had Done $1M/Month Before"   **NÚMEROS:** Equipe toda jovem, zero experiência com $1M/mês   **VISUAL:** Desafio de credibilidade: convencer equipe sem histórico próprio de sucesso nesse nível   [01:11:35] Um especially when no one in your organization has seen numbers like that before. Like our whole team is young as fuck. Like none of us have made a million dollars a month.   [01:11:55] Like if you think about someone like Jeremy when he comes into an operation he's like all right you guys can do a million a month. They know like that's true because he's done it before. He knows what's probable to make it happen.   [01:12:20] Um and stuff like that, but like when no one has done it before, he's never made a million dollars a month at this point. I've never made a million dollars. We're just like, "All right, let's make a million dollars a month."   [01:12:40] Um it's difficult for people to like believe that. And I think that that was a big part of it is actually getting them to believe it. And again, showing them numbers and how probable it actually is made a huge difference.   [01:13:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Culture Tip: Money Monday Calls"   **NÚMEROS:** N/A   **VISUAL:** Ritual semanal toda segunda-feira para alinhar equipe e criar momentum   [01:13:10] Um we always do like just a little tip like on culture. I think one of our strong suits is actually like team culture. We have a really really strong culture. Like everyone genuinely wants to work every day.   [01:13:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Hunger Games Gamification"   **NÚMEROS:** Cash prizes, VCs separados no Discord até 1 AM grinding   **VISUAL:** Sistema de draft de times, competição entre equipes, incentivos diários, live spin wheel   [01:13:40] Uh we always do like a huge Money Monday call every Monday and we set up like Hunger Games. This is our first million dollar month we started doing this.   [01:14:00] We would do like a live spin. We would put everyone's name on it. Um we would make them they would get like drafted to teams and we make those teams compete against each other.   [01:14:20] And these guys were grinding. Like we just gamified the whole process. Like these guys were like in we would give them like we use Discord for internals.   [01:14:40] We would give them their own like VCs for different teams and these guys would be in there till like 1:00 AM grinding every single day. Um to like beat other teams.   [01:15:00] And we gave huge like cash prizes to these guys and we would do incentives like every day. Everyone was just really dialed in to hit this goal.   [01:15:20] And I think making the closers and just fueling into competition uh played a huge role especially with the funnel we were running with like a ton of outbound stuff.   [01:15:40] Our outbound team is huge. Um, we need these guys to be like really really on it because we don't give them access to a scheduler. So there's no calls just being booked. Like we have to go out and actually book the calls ourselves.   [01:16:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Accountability System"   **NÚMEROS:** N/A   **VISUAL:** Consequências: Late to meetings = lose priority on leads, miss KPI = nerfeado em leads qualificados, Hunger Games team accountability   [01:16:10] Um, on top of that, we just doubled all KPIs as long as it's possible obviously and simply put up with less like being late to meetings, missing KPI.   [01:16:30] Like they were being held accountable by their own little like Hunger Games team and um they would get nerfed on leads.   [01:16:45] Like if they show up late, you don't get priority on like the good applications. They don't get priority on good leads. Um, and we just raised everyone's standard. And yeah.   [01:17:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Reason #3: Closing 2-3 Star Leads (Not Just 4-5)"   **NÚMEROS:** $0-$500K = apenas 4-5 star leads (orgânico, YouTube, hard qualified)   **VISUAL:** Mudança de mentalidade: parar de chamar tudo de "unqualified", desenvolver backend selling tactics   [01:17:10] Um, reason number three, putting in the effort to close two and three star leads, not just four and five star leads, was massive for us.   [01:17:30] Um, from 0 to 500K a month, we were practically only closing four and five star leads. We were closing organic, YouTube, and hard qualified leads. Like that is the most goated setup you could possibly have.   [01:18:00] Um, and naturally our sales team got accustomed to this and deemed every other lead that wasn't at that level of intent unqualified.   [01:18:20] Um, but we like built this culture around that and it was really really fucked. So in order to hit bigger numbers, we had to learn different back-end selling tactics uh to be able to bring up two and three star leads to a four and five levels of intent uh which are the buyers.   [01:18:50] Um, and we also just had to fire a lot of guys like a lot of guys could not make that adjustment um from just calling every lead unqualified just because they weren't like a laydown deal that I've been watching Brez for 4 months.   [01:19:15] Um it was just like a hard uh culture shift, but we made it happen.   [01:19:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Backend Selling Tactics"   **NÚMEROS:** N/A   **VISUAL:** Lista de táticas: Hammer Them para qualified typeform apps (não só booked calls), FAQ breakout videos, ROAZ vídeos do segundo canal, engagement bait para identificar curiosidades   [01:19:35] Um so just a little insight on the different tactics we used on the back end. Hammer Them was definitely huge. Um but we didn't use Hammer Them just for booked calls.   [01:19:55] We used Hammer Them for all qualified type form apps. So like we struggled with connection rate at times, meaning like people would submit an application and we couldn't get in touch with them.   [01:20:20] So, we actually ran Hammer Them to everyone that was qualified and this boosted our connection rate and show rate, not just booked calls, which was pretty cool.   [01:20:40] Um, FAQ breakout videos, those were huge on the confirmation page. Um, and the ROAZ videos on his second channel, those were used uh pre-call for every single person.   [01:21:05] So, we would kind of like engagement bait them to see what they were curious the most about, and then we would just dish them off uh the videos that they're curious about.   [01:21:25] And uh he would crank those out, and those were huge, too.   [01:21:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Reason #4: Multiple Conversion Mechanisms"   **NÚMEROS:** Webinar adicionou $475K em topline revenue imediatamente   **VISUAL:** Antes: só VSSL funnel | Depois: VSSL + Monthly warm webinar = $1M breakthrough   [01:21:40] Um, reason number four, adding multiple conversion mechanisms. We were only running that VSSL funnel. Uh, then we added a monthly warm webinar and immediately added $475,000 in topline revenue.   [01:22:10] Shout out last mastermind. We I came to that mastermind and then just immediately started working on that webinar. Did it the next month. What month was that mastermind?   [01:22:30] May.   [01:22:35] Yeah. May we did a million dollars because we ran the webinar. So, uh, we ran this webinar to the same offer, same, uh, like type form to outbound schedule.   [01:22:55] Um, and some people just need different ways to convert. That's what we realized. Like not everyone wants to go through like a weird sales call with some 19-year-old closer that just came out of Jeremy Miner training.   [01:23:20] Like some people just need to like just go through a different process. So yeah, and webinars are just insane. Like webinars are literally cracked.   [01:23:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Retargeting Paid Ads Strategy"   **NÚMEROS:** Easy ROAS attribution + offer awareness massiva   **VISUAL:** Benefício duplo: Direct attribution de cash + Awareness que ele TEM uma oferta (muitos não sabiam)   [01:23:45] So another important thing we started doing was running retargeting paid um paid ads uh retargeting on YouTube and Meta.   [01:24:05] We did not only did this directly attribute a ton of cash with easy ROAS but it helped immensely with offer awareness.   [01:24:25] Um I think a lot of people especially when they come from an organic background just like you probably heard it in his tone like he doesn't want to be salesy. He doesn't want to pitch. He doesn't want to um like talk about his offer or anything. He doesn't want to get called a guru.   [01:24:50] Um it's really really common. But you'd be surprised in his head, he thinks that everyone's thinking about how he's selling an offer, but you'd be surprised at how little people actually knew he had an offer, especially with his type of content.   [01:25:15] Um, like the lifestyle top of funnel shit. We would get on the phone with people and they'd be like, "What is this? Like, what are you talking about?"   [01:25:35] Um, so the ads actually helped a lot with just offer awareness in general and just let them know that he actually had shit they can buy.   [01:25:55] In his head, he's thinking, "Oh my god, these guys think I'm a sellout, etc." In reality, people are just casually watching him. They had no clue he had stuff to buy.   [01:26:15] So even if it wasn't like directly attributed from the ads, like they booked in there, etc. They would actually come back and like know there's something in his bio from the YouTube video and go and click it and buy that way.   [01:26:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Retargeting Ads = Another Conversion Mechanism"   **NÚMEROS:** N/A   **VISUAL:** Ads super diretos apresentando oferta = awareness que você tem algo para vender   [01:26:45] Um, also just like super direct retargeting ads presenting your offer are great and let people know you actually have shit they can buy.   [01:27:05] Like I said, these people often buy from organic content because they saw your ad and are now aware you have an offer. Um, I would consider that another conversion mechanism as well.   [01:27:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Reason #5: Do Everything to Make Money We Wouldn't Otherwise Make"   **NÚMEROS:** N/A   **VISUAL:** Mindset shift: NUNCA dizer "não vale o tempo", fazer TUDO que gera dinheiro adicional   [01:27:35] Um, reason number four [na verdade #5], the last reason, we just did everything we could to make money we wouldn't otherwise make.   [01:27:55] Never once did anyone on the team say the words like, "That's not worth the time." If it was going to make us money that we would not otherwise make, we always did it.   [01:28:15] Um, we were completely bought into hitting the goal and everyone on the team was prepared to do everything in their power to make more money that we would not otherwise make.   [01:28:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Examples: Extra Money Activities"   **NÚMEROS:** 1-2x/semana group sales calls   **VISUAL:** Lista: Group sales calls para non-closes (pós-webinar), selfie videos personalizados, qualquer coisa que gere dinheiro extra   [01:28:45] So, just like some quick little things that I think most people would bitch about doing or like someone like Brez would bitch about doing but he didn't was like those group sales calls for non-closes, especially after a webinar.   [01:29:10] So, everyone that was a non-close that hopped on a sales call, we would have them do like a group sales call. Um, so he would hop on with them and he would just like handle their objections.   [01:29:30] And then immediately he wouldn't even pitch on it. He would just have the sales rep that was on the phone with them reach out to them and we made a lot of money doing that. We'd do that one to two times a week.   [01:29:55] Um, and on top of that, people who just needed like selfie videos, for example, uh, like Jeremy does, like just have Brez send him a quick video of people who are like on edge just to push them over the line.   [01:30:15] But moral of the story, we were just doing anything to make more money. And I think just that mindset shift in general within the team uh, made a huge difference.   [01:30:35] We weren't bitching about doing anything that was going to make us money. But yeah, that's all I have. I believe that's that. Sure. Q&A. Any questions?   [01:30:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── **TEXTO:** "Q&A Session - Pierre"   **NÚMEROS:** N/A   **VISUAL:** Sessão de perguntas e respostas sobre paid ads e operações   [01:31:00] [Pergunta sobre diferença entre 2-3 star vs 4-5 star leads]   [01:31:05] What's the difference for you between two and three star leads versus four and five stars?   [01:31:15] Um just people who weren't ready to buy. They just were more curious. They didn't know exactly what was going on.   [01:31:30] Um maybe they were just like they hop on a call for example and they were just like, "Oh, I just need more information." Like they clearly are not going to buy.   [01:31:50] Then our closer would just get pissed off and just kick them off. Um, and so it's pretty easy to figure out who's ready to buy because they were just used to laydowns.   [01:32:10] They're used to these guys that are just ready to buy because his content's so cracked and the process is so cracked.   [01:32:30] [Pergunta sobre estrutura das group sales calls]   [01:32:35] How do you run those group sales calls? What do you do?   [01:32:45] I just... Is this on? I don't know. Hello.   [01:32:55] All right. Anyways, um, pretty simple, bro. I would just get on there and say, "All right, who's got questions for me?" Like it was simple, bro. I would literally just get on there and say that and they just...   [01:33:15] Bro, they're like ecstatic over the fact they're on a call with me. They're like, "Oh, oh my gosh." So, they're throwing questions in the chat.   [01:33:35] Sometimes though, if there wasn't a lot of people in the calls, I'd be quiet and I'd have to get them going. But most of the time, it's just like they're fighting for who can unmute their mic first to ask me a question.   [01:33:55] And I just answer their question, but like in the back of my mind, if it's a question that I know kind of is an objection to buying, I kind of try to handle that objection while also giving them like free value.   [01:34:20] Got it.   [01:34:25] But like it just builds goodwill. Even if they don't close, it's insane market research. Like it's the deepest in the funnel you could get. Um they feed you your breakout videos, they feed you your ads, like they feed you every objection possible.   [01:34:50] Cool. Thanks, bro.   [01:34:55] [Pergunta sobre framing dos convites para group calls]   [01:35:00] How do you frame those like invites?   [01:35:05] Um, we have the closer reach out and then be like, "Hey, Brez is hosting a few uh a free Q&A about the program itself. Um, if you want to hop on, it's completely free. I'll just answer some of your questions and clarify some stuff. It's at Wednesday at 7 PM." Send them the Zoom link.   [01:35:35] Yeah.   [01:35:40] [Pergunta sobre setup de Hammer Them]   [01:35:45] So, you said you had a 30 second you can call your people within 30 seconds. You said your sales team outbound's pretty dialed. What's the setup that you have that you can call in 30 seconds? What CRM are you using and how do you get someone to call them?   [01:36:15] We just use GoHighLevel, but we use not um we have notifications to our Discord, which is our internal platform.   [01:36:35] Um and we just have a ton of setters, bro. We have a ton of outbound setters and they literally spawn. They get like it's called like IC app.   [01:36:55] We just spawn that channel. As soon as they come in, they call them and then when they called them, they just leave like a little emoji on them to let them know that they were already called.   [01:37:15] How many setters you got on your team?   [01:37:20] Um, right now we hired a lot for the webinar. We cut down a bit. I think right now we have maybe 30 setters, 25 setters. Yeah.   [01:37:40] Dude. We have so many sales people that I'll get notifications on my phone of Xavier just closed X amount. I'll be like, who the fuck is Xavier?   [01:38:00] Like I don't even like we're trying to shift out of that um like the outbound stuff because it's too reliant and but we're testing it with paid. We're trying to get it with paid like just as a test versus like schedulers and stuff.   [01:38:25] [Múltiplas perguntas adicionais sobre pickup rates, compensação de setters, webinar spend, KPIs, etc.]   [Continua Q&A até o final...]   [01:02:42] [FIM]   ---   ## 📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO   **NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:**   **Crescimento e Revenue:** - $0 → $500K/mês sem paid ads - $0 → $1M+/mês total - Primeiro $1M em maio 2024 - Último mês: $1M+ novamente - Pico anterior: $544K em janeiro - Estagnação: 4 meses entre $400-500K - Webinar adicionou: $475K em topline revenue imediatamente - Começou com 4K followers em março 2024   **Métricas de Conteúdo:** - 43,000+ pessoas na comunidade de clipping - ~2,000 clippers ativos - $50K/mês investimento em clippers - $2/1K views pagamento para clippers ($1 normal, $2 em campanhas especiais) - 1 bilhão+ views estimados (clippers + orgânico) - 60M profile views/30 dias (Instagram apenas) - 100K novos followers/30 dias - 550K TikTok followers - 650K Instagram followers - 32K novos subs YouTube/30 dias - 650K novos viewers YouTube/30 dias - 10K subs segundo canal/28 dias - 550K total viewers segundo canal - 400M views em 1 mês (GT3 RS purchase) - 1.3M+ likes em clips virais individuais   **Métricas de Vendas:** - Offer: $4,800 (antes $4,000) - $33.3K/dia necessário para $1M/mês - 85% show rate - $1,000 avg CC por call taken - 40 calls/dia necessários (KPI real: 60 calls/dia) - 476 deals/mês necessários - $2,100 avg deal size - 30 segundos speed to lead - 60% connection rate (com Hammer Them) - ~50% response rate a textos - 9 closers atuais - 25-30 setters   **Webinar:** - Primeiro webinar: $15K spend - Segundo webinar: ~$50K spend - ROAS: 3.7-4.0x - 10 dias promo orgânico - 15 dias promo paid - 1x/mês frequência   **Posting Frequency:** - YouTube main: 1 vídeo a cada 5 dias (20-60 min) - YouTube segundo: 1 vídeo/semana - Instagram: 2-4 posts/dia - TikTok: 1-3 posts/dia - Centenas de clips/dia (via clippers)   **ROI Estimado:** - Clippers: 5-8x ROI estimado (não rastreável precisamente)   **ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:**   **1. Sistema de Clip Pages ($50K/mês):** - 43K pessoas na comunidade Whop - 2K clippers ativos pagos por view - Comissão: $1-2 por 1K views - Campanhas especiais com CPM aumentado - Redistribuição massiva em theme pages - Brand awareness orgânica e viral - Reciclar content para main pages   **2. Funil de Conteúdo Estratégico:** - **TOFU:** Clip pages (bilhões de views, awareness) - **MOFU:** Short form TikTok/Instagram (milhões/dia) - **BOFU:** Long form YouTube (20-60 min, conexão profunda)   **3. Estratégia de Dois Canais YouTube:** - **Main Channel:** Lifestyle/influencer, 1 soft CTA para segundo canal, zero mention de offers, entretenimento puro - **Second Channel:** Educational/business, harder CTAs, direct VSSL funnel, value-driven   **4. 5 Tipos de Conteúdo:** - Educational (segundo canal) - Motivational (life advice, comparações) - Lifestyle (jets, jet skis, flexing estratégico) - Humorous (memes virais: Fiji Water grandpa, Turkey hair transplant) - Cars (compras virais massivas)   **5. Memes Virais Estratégicos:** - Fiji Water grandpa (1M+ likes, deflection de "guru allegations") - Turkey hair transplant (top comment em quase todos os vídeos) - In-N-Out "nice rental" (1.3M likes) - TJR boxing match collab   **6. 4 Razões Para $1M/Mês:**   **#1: Fizeram a Matemática (Money Math)** - Breakdown completo: $33.3K/dia, 40 calls, 476 deals - KPI buffer: 60 calls/dia (não 40) - Criatividade com cash collection - Foco em AOV e recurring payments   **#2: Elevaram Padrões da Equipe** - Buy-in total no objetivo comum - Hunger Games gamification - Money Monday calls semanais - Draft de times competindo - Cash prizes - Discord VCs até 1 AM - Zero tolerância: late, miss KPI = nerfed em leads - Accountability por peer teams   **#3: Fecharam 2-3 Star Leads** - Antes: só 4-5 star laydowns - Desenvolveram backend selling tactics - Hammer Them para qualified typeform apps (não só booked calls) - FAQ breakout videos - ROAZ vídeos do segundo canal - Engagement bait para identificar curiosidades - Fired muitos closers que não se adaptaram   **#4: Múltiplos Mecanismos de Conversão** - VSSL funnel (opt-in free training) - Monthly warm webinar (+$475K imediato) - Retargeting paid ads (YouTube + Meta) - Offer awareness ads (muitos não sabiam que ele vendia algo)   **#5: Fizeram TUDO Para Dinheiro Extra** - Group sales calls 1-2x/semana (non-closes) - Selfie videos personalizados - Nunca disseram "não vale o tempo" - Mindset: fazer qualquer coisa que gere $ adicional   **7. Funnel Setup:** - Opt-in VSSL (não scheduler) - Typeform qualification - "Vamos entrar em contato" (não auto-schedule) - Setter reach out <30 segundos - Hard qualification (demographic jovem) - Outbound-heavy (transitioning para scheduler com paid)   **8. Backend Selling Tactics:** - Hammer Them para ALL qualified apps - Boost connection rate e show rate - FAQ breakout videos na confirmation page - Pre-call ROAZ vídeos - Engagement bait para customizar nurture   **9. Retargeting Strategy:** - Direct ROAS attribution - Massive offer awareness (problema: ninguém sabia que ele vendia) - YouTube + Meta retargeting - Super raw ad creatives (Brez segurando câmera)   **10. Group Sales Call Strategy:** - Non-closes da última semana - Brez entra e responde perguntas - Objection handling disfarçado de free value - Zero pitch direto (closers follow up depois) - Insane market research - Feeds breakout videos, ads, objections   **ESTRUTURA:**   **Equipe:** - 9 closers (cortando para 8) - 25-30 setters - 1 lead setter - 1 lead closer - Full-time filmer (Brez) - Equipe de segundo canal YouTube (recém trocada) - 43K clippers (2K ativos)   **Ferramentas:** - Whop (clipping community) - GoHighLevel (CRM) - Discord (internals, VCs, notifications) - IC app (spawning) - Typeform (applications) - ManyChat/DMs (nurturing)   **Compensação:** - Setters: comissão apenas (via programa de Pierre) - Clippers: $1-2 per 1K views - Closers: comissão (detalhes não mencionados) - Performance-based tudo   **Cultura:** - Money Monday calls semanais - Hunger Games gamification - Discord VCs por team - Grinding até 1 AM comum - Zero tolerância para atrasos/miss KPI - Peer accountability forte - Buy-in total em meta coletiva   **DADOS FINANCEIROS:**   **Revenue:** - $1M+ CC último mês - $1.17M CC em maio (primeiro $1M) - $544K pico pré-mastermind (janeiro) - $400-500K plateau por 4 meses - $0 → $500K sem ads - Webinar: +$475K topline imediato   **Custos:** - $50K/mês em clippers - $15K primeiro webinar - $50K segundo webinar   **Métricas:** - $1,000 avg CC per call taken - $2,100 avg deal size - $4,800 price point - 85% show rate - 60% connection rate (com Hammer Them) - 3.7-4.0x ROAS (webinar paid) - 5-8x ROI estimado (clippers, não rastreável)   **Target:** - $33.3K/dia = $1M/mês - 40 calls/dia necessário (KPI: 60) - 476 deals/mês necessário   --- **DURAÇÃO:** 01:02:42 **FORMATO:** Apresentação ao vivo com troféu $1M/mês + Q&A dupla (Brez organic + Pierre paid)
